The resulting update reduces to inversions of fixed low-rank matrices and requires no additional backward passes. Empirically, our approach matches the editing quality of strong baselines on the main KE metrics while accelerating the editing procedure by up to 6x, owing to the batched MEMIT-style formulation and the low-dimensional inversions enabled by the Woodbury identity. These results show that closed-form, parameter-modifying KE can be extended efficiently beyond dense layers, opening a path toward scalable knowledge editing in modern sparse LLM architectures.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a framework for knowledge editing in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs, enhancing editing speed by up to 6x without compromising quality.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the limitations of existing knowledge editing methods which primarily focus on dense layers, leaving MoE-based LLMs without effective editing tools despite their growing prevalence.
AI.method: The authors propose a MEMIT-like framework that uses the tensor structure of MoE layers for editing at the expert level, employing the Woodbury matrix identity to efficiently manage expert weight updates without full matrix inversion.
AI.result: Their approach achieves comparable editing quality to established baselines while accelerating the process by up to 6 times due to optimized formulations and low-rank matrix manipulation.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates the feasibility of efficient knowledge editing in sparse LLM architectures, potentially enhancing the scalability and effectiveness of such models.

title: PPAI: Enabling Personalized LLM Agent Interoperability for Collaborative Edge Intelligence
summary: Deploying large language model (LLM) on edge device enables personalized LLM agents for various users. The growing availability of diverse personalized agents presents a unique opportunity for peer-to-peer (P2P) collaboration, wherein each user can delegate tasks beyond the local agent's expertise to remote agents more suited for the specific query. This paper introduces PPAI, the first personalized LLM agent interoperability system, which enables users to collaborate with each other based on agent specialization. However, the ever-changing pool of agents and their interchangeable capacity introduce new challenges when it comes to matching queries to agents and balancing loads, compared with existing P2P systems. Therefore, we propose a scalable query-agent pair scoring mechanism based on prototypes to identify suitable agents within a P2P network with churn. Moreover, we propose a multi-agent interoperability Bayesian game to balance local demand and global efficiency, when changes in remote agent load occur too quickly to be observed. Finally, we implement a prototype of PPAI and demonstrate that it substantially broadens the range of tasks that could be carried out while maintaining load balance. On average, it achieves an accuracy improvement of up to 7.96% across multiple tasks, while reducing latency by 16.34% compared to the baseline.

AI.tldr: This paper presents PPAI, a system for personalized LLM agent interoperability that facilitates user collaboration by connecting specialized agents, addressing challenges related to task delegation in dynamic P2P networks.
AI.motivation: The increase in personalized LLM agents allows for enhanced collaboration, enabling users to delegate tasks to agents better suited for specific queries, but it presents challenges in managing agent availability and query matching.
AI.method: The authors introduce a scalable query-agent pair scoring mechanism based on prototypes to identify suitable agents. Additionally, a multi-agent interoperability Bayesian game is proposed to manage local demand and global efficiency amidst quickly changing agent loads.
AI.result: PPAI's implementation shows substantial enhancements, achieving an accuracy improvement of up to 7.96% and reducing latency by 16.34% across various tasks compared to existing systems.
AI.conclusion: PPAI significantly expands the capability for task execution in a decentralized manner while maintaining efficiency and balance within the system.

title: NeuroLiDAR: Adaptive Frame Rate Depth Sensing via Neuromorphic Event-LiDAR Fusion
summary: LiDARs are widely used for 3D depth reconstruction, but their performance is often limited by inherent hardware constraints that impose trade-offs between range, spatial resolution, and frame rate. Many LiDAR systems typically operate at low frame rates (e.g., 5-10 Hz), prioritizing long-range sensing over responsiveness to rapid scene changes. We present NeuroLiDAR, an adaptive depth sensing framework that achieves effective frame rates of up to $\approx$66 Hz by fusing temporally sparse LiDAR data with temporally dense inputs from neuromorphic event cameras. NeuroLiDAR integrates two components: event-based keyframe detection and event-guided depth extrapolation, to dynamically adjust the sensing rate in response to scene dynamics. To evaluate our approach, we introduce ELiDAR, a dataset spanning outdoor and indoor scenarios, and show that NeuroLiDAR reduces depth reconstruction error by $\approx$29\% in RMSE while achieving adaptive frame rates between 27.8-47.3 Hz. Our code and dataset are available at https://github.com/darshanakgr/neurolidar.

AI.tldr: NeuroLiDAR is an adaptive depth sensing framework that enhances LiDAR performance by fusing sparse data with neuromorphic cameras, achieving high frame rates and improved depth accuracy.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of traditional LiDAR systems constrained by hardware trade-offs, optimizing for both responsiveness and depth accuracy.
AI.method: NeuroLiDAR employs event-based keyframe detection and event-guided depth extrapolation to dynamically adjust sensing rates based on scene dynamics.
AI.result: NeuroLiDAR demonstrates reduced depth reconstruction error by approximately 29% RMSE and adaptability in frame rates between 27.8-47.3 Hz.
AI.conclusion: NeuroLiDAR significantly enhances depth sensing performance, offering a flexible solution for dynamic environments.

title: KVDrive: A Holistic Multi-Tier KV Cache Management System for Long-Context LLM Inference
summary: Supporting long-context LLMs is challenging due to the substantial memory demands of the key-value (KV) cache. Existing offloading systems store the full cache in host memory and selectively fetch critical entries during decoding, but this strategy quickly hits a ceiling: sparsity cannot be pushed further without degrading accuracy. As a result, when context length and batch size grow, the volume of KV transfers rises sharply and becomes the dominant source of decoding latency. We present KVDrive, a holistic multi-tier KV cache management system spanning GPU memory, host DRAM, and SSD. Unlike prior work that pursues greater sparsity through algorithmic refinements, KVDrive tackles the problem from a systems perspective - jointly orchestrating cache placement, pipeline scheduling, and cross-tier coordination to sustain high-throughput inference under tight GPU budgets. KVDrive advances three fundamental capabilities: it adapts cache management to attention behavior to maximize reuse and minimize redundant data movement; it restructures the decoding pipeline to overlap I/O- and CPU/GPU compute-bound stages, eliminating stalls across heterogeneous resources; and it harmonizes data movement across memory tiers to unlock scalable long-context inference far beyond GPU and DRAM limits. We have implemented a fully functional prototype of KVDrive and evaluated it on long-context benchmarks with popular LLMs. The system achieves up to 1.74x higher throughput compared to state-of-the-art works while preserving accuracy.

AI.tldr: KVDrive is a multi-tier cache management system designed to optimize long-context LLMs by improving throughput and minimizing latency during decoding.
AI.motivation: The research addresses the issue of high latency in long-context LLMs caused by memory demands and inefficiencies in existing KV cache management systems, especially as context length and batch size increase.
AI.method: KVDrive utilizes a multi-tier approach to cache management across GPU memory, host DRAM, and SSD. It focuses on cache placement, pipeline scheduling, and cross-tier coordination to enhance performance.
AI.result: The implementation of KVDrive achieves up to 1.74x higher throughput compared to current state-of-the-art systems while maintaining accuracy during long-context inference.
AI.conclusion: KVDrive effectively supports long-context LLMs by optimizing resource usage and reducing latency, thus enabling scalable inference beyond traditional memory limits.

title: CLAP: Contrastive Latent-space Prompt Optimization for End-to-end Autonomous Driving
summary: End-to-end autonomous driving systems powered by Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models achieve strong performance on common driving scenarios, yet remain brittle in rare but safety-critical long-tail situations such as active construction zones and complex yielding geometries. In this paper, we present a method that addresses the long-tail challenging scenes beyond data scaling and model training. We introduce CLAP (Contrastive Latent-space Prompt optimization), a location-aware adaptation framework that augments a frozen VLA driving model with per-roadblock soft prompts, optimized from crowdsourced data and retrieved on demand via Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication. Our approach rests on two observations from VLAs' latent space: (i) at the VLA's hidden-state layer, scenarios from the same roadblock cluster tightly and occupy compact regions of the latent space; and (ii) within a single roadblock, long-tail and normal frames are heavily intermixed in the latent representation, making it difficult to improve one without disturbing the other. CLAP addresses this via a two-stage pipeline: supervised contrastive learning to discover a roadblock-specific hard-scene direction, followed by directionally regularized prompt optimization that selectively improves challenging frames while preserving normal frame performance. On the NAVSIM benchmark with various state-of-the-art VLA backbones, CLAP reduces challenging scenario planning error by 24% with no regression on normal frames, significantly improving planning performance.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CLAP, a method for improving autonomous driving systems in rare, safety-critical scenarios by optimizing prompts from crowdsourced data, resulting in a 24% reduction in planning errors for challenging scenarios without degrading performance on normal frames.
AI.motivation: To enhance the performance of autonomous driving systems in rare but critical driving situations that are not sufficiently addressed by existing data scaling and model training techniques.
AI.method: CLAP employs a location-aware adaptation framework that utilizes crowdsourced data to optimize soft prompts for a frozen Vision-Language-Action model, using a two-stage pipeline involving supervised contrastive learning and directionally regularized prompt optimization.
AI.result: By implementing CLAP, the paper reports a 24% reduction in planning errors for challenging roadblock scenarios tested on the NAVSIM benchmark, while maintaining performance on standard driving conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses the performance gap in handling long-tail situations in autonomous driving without sacrificing the system's capability in standard scenarios.

title: StableVLA: Towards Robust Vision-Language-Action Models without Extra Data
comment: Accepted by ICML 2026. Code: https://github.com/DAGroup-PKU/HumanNet. Project website: https://dagroup-pku.github.io/StableVLA/
summary: It is infeasible to encompass all possible disturbances within the training dataset. This raises a critical question regarding the robustness of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models when encountering unseen real-world visual disturbances, particularly under imperfect visual conditions. In this work, we conduct a systematic study based on recent state-of-the-art VLA models and reveal a significant performance drop when visual disturbances absent from the training data are introduced. To mitigate this issue, we propose a lightweight adapter module grounded in information theory, termed the Information Bottleneck Adapter (IB-Adapter), which selectively filters potential noise from visual inputs. Without requiring any extra data or augmentation strategies, IB-Adapter consistently improves over the baseline by an average of 30%, while adding fewer than 10M parameters, demonstrating notable efficiency and effectiveness. Furthermore, even with a 14x smaller backbone (0.5B parameters) and no pre-training on the Open X-Embodiment dataset, our model StableVLA achieves robustness competitive with 7B-scale state-of-the-art VLAs. With negligible parameter overhead (<10M), our approach maintains accuracy on long-horizon tasks and surpasses OpenPi under both synthetic and physical visual corruptions.

AI.tldr: This work addresses the performance challenges of VLA models under unseen visual disturbances by introducing IB-Adapter, which enhances robustness without extra data.
AI.motivation: To improve the robustness of VLA models against unseen visual disturbances that are not present in training.
AI.method: A lightweight adapter grounded in information theory (IB-Adapter) filters noise from visual inputs, enhancing model performance without extra data.
AI.result: IB-Adapter improves baseline performance by an average of 30% with fewer than 10M additional parameters, showing efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed model, StableVLA, achieves competitive robustness with fewer parameters and no pre-training, outperforming existing methods under various visual corruptions.

title: DecoRec: Decomposed 3D Scene Reconstruction from Single-View Images via Object-Level Diffusion
summary: In this paper, we introduce \textit{DecoRec}, a novel system designed to elevate single-view 2D images to a decomposed 3D scene mesh. Current methods for single-view scene reconstruction typically rely on object retrieval or the regression of coarse 3D voxels or surfaces, leading to inaccuracies in capturing the appearance and geometry of the input image. The lack of high-quality large-scale scene-level datasets further complicates direct 3D scene generation from single-view images. To achieve high-quality 3D scene generation from a single-view image, DecoRec takes advantage of recent diffusion-based single-view object reconstruction methods to reconstruct individual objects separately. Subsequently, a refinement pipeline is proposed to effectively merge these reconstructed objects, enhancing appearance and geometry through a differentiable rendering technique and diffusion-guided refinement. Our results demonstrate that DecoRec facilitates high-quality single-view scene reconstruction in both geometry and novel synthesis, offering significant benefits for downstream applications like room interior design.

AI.tldr: DecoRec is a system that improves 3D scene reconstruction from single-view 2D images by decomposing the scene into individual objects and refining their appearance and geometry.
AI.motivation: Current single-view scene reconstruction methods are inaccurate due to reliance on object retrieval or coarse regression techniques and suffer from a lack of quality datasets.
AI.method: DecoRec utilizes diffusion-based object reconstruction for separate object handling followed by a refinement pipeline that merges these objects using differentiable rendering and diffusion-guided techniques.
AI.result: DecoRec achieves high-quality reconstruction in both geometry and novel view synthesis, surpassing existing methods.
AI.conclusion: DecoRec provides significant advantages for applications such as room interior design by enhancing the standard of single-view scene reconstruction.

title: A Data-Efficient Path to Multilingual LLMs: Language Expansion via Post-training PARAM$Δ$ Integration into Upcycled MoE
summary: Expanding Large Language Models~(LLMs) to new languages is a costly endeavor, demanding extensive Continued Pre-Training~(CPT) and data-intensive alignment. While recent data-free merging techniques attempt to bypass alignment by fusing a multilingual CPT-enhanced model with its instruct counterpart, they are plagued by a critical trade-off: mitigating parameter conflicts to preserve original abilities inevitably dilutes new language acquisition, and vice-versa. To resolve this conflict, we introduce \method, which upcycles a dense model into a Mixture-of-Experts~(MoE) architecture, allocating different experts to different languages. Alignment ability is then transferred by grafting a MoE-expanded parameter delta~($Δ_{\text{post}}$) to the CPT-enhanced base model, bypassing the complex alignment phase. Experiments demonstrate \method's superiority even against baselines with similar FLOPs or number of parameters; it improves performance on expanded languages while effectively preserving original capabilities. We further show our approach is highly applicable across different models and Post-training deltas.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a method to enhance Large Language Models (LLMs) for new languages by using a Mixture-of-Experts architecture, which allows for better language adaptation without sacrificing original model capabilities.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges and trade-offs involved in expanding LLMs to new languages, which typically require extensive retraining and alignment processes.
AI.method: The proposed method introduces a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, which uses different experts for different languages and employs a parameter delta method to transfer alignment ability without the need for complex alignment.
AI.result: Experiments show that the proposed method outperforms baseline models with similar resource usage, effectively improving performance on newly added languages while maintaining the original capabilities of the model.
AI.conclusion: The approach is versatile and applicable to various models, showing significant improvements in multilingual expansion while preserving existing functionalities.

title: Your SaaS Is an Insurance Product: A Modeling Framework
comment: 23 pages, 2 figures, 7 tables. Companion code archived at DOI 10.5281/zenodo.20213155
summary: Capped-usage SaaS products -- LLM subscriptions such as Claude Code and ChatGPT, cloud platforms such as Vercel and Cloudflare Workers, corporate benefit platforms, identity-verification services with liability transfer -- share a structural signature with insurance products: a fixed premium decoupled from realized consumption, stochastic per-user demand with heavy-tailed severity, a non-fungible cap that resets on a fixed schedule, and a portfolio-level exposure that requires reserve adequacy under tail risk. We argue that this is not an analogy. It is the same operational problem actuarial science has been tooled for decades to address, restated with new dependent variables (tokens, bandwidth bytes, function-invocations, gym check-ins) in place of medical claims. This paper proposes a modeling framework for capped-usage SaaS pricing built from frequency-severity decomposition, premium calculation principles, and Monte Carlo reserve adequacy. We map the framework to publicly observable subscription tiers in two domains (LLM services and cloud platforms), ground it in canonical health-insurance economics (Arrow 1963; Pauly 1968; Manning et al. 1987; Brot-Goldberg et al. 2017), and demonstrate divergence from traditional unit economics through a worked example. The contribution is operational rather than theoretical: not a new theorem, but vocabulary and tools currently absent from cs.LG/stat.ML practice.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a modeling framework for capped-usage SaaS pricing, drawing parallels with actuarial science principles, emphasizing the operational aspects rather than theoretical advancements.
AI.motivation: To address the structural similarities between capped-usage SaaS products and insurance products, examining how actuarial science methodologies can aid in pricing models for SaaS.
AI.method: The framework incorporates frequency-severity decomposition, premium calculation principles, and Monte Carlo simulations for reserve adequacy, mapping it to subscription tiers in LLM services and cloud platforms.
AI.result: The framework provides insights into the pricing of SaaS products and illustrates differences from traditional unit economics through a worked example.
AI.conclusion: The paper focuses on operational contributions, offering tools and vocabulary to enhance the understanding and application of actuarial principles within the context of capped-usage SaaS.

title: PH-Dreamer: A Physics-Driven World Model via Port-Hamiltonian Generative Dynamics
summary: World models built on recurrent state space architectures enable efficient latent imagination, yet remain physically unstructured, producing dynamics that violate conservation and dissipative principles. We introduce a unified Port-Hamiltonian framework that remedies this through three synergistic mechanisms. First, we embed implicit physical priors into recurrent transitions by modeling projected latent evolution as action controlled energy routing governed by flow and dissipation, biasing the projected PH phase space toward a more compact and physically structured representation. Second, we develop a kinematics aware energy world model that estimates the Hamiltonian and power balance from proprioceptive observations, providing an explicit physical signal for thermodynamic reasoning. Third, leveraging these energy gradients, we establish an energy guided Actor-Critic that uses Lagrangian multipliers to regularize policy optimization toward lower energy and smoother control. Across visual control benchmarks, this paradigm not only attains superior asymptotic returns but also elevates internal simulator fidelity by establishing a tighter, lower variance alignment between imagined and real rewards, all while reducing latent phase space volume by 4.18-8.41%, energy consumption by up to 7.80%, and mean squared jerk by up to 9.38%.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a Port-Hamiltonian framework to enhance world models using recurrent state space architectures by embedding physical principles, improving energy modeling, and optimizing control policies, resulting in better performance on visual control tasks.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing world models that violate physical conservation laws and produce unrealistic dynamics during latent imagination.
AI.method: A unified Port-Hamiltonian framework is introduced that includes implicit physical priors, a kinematics-aware energy world model, and an energy-guided Actor-Critic approach utilizing Lagrangian multipliers for policy optimization.
AI.result: The proposed framework achieves improved asymptotic returns on visual control benchmarks, with better alignment between imagined and real rewards, as well as reductions in latent phase space volume, energy consumption, and mean squared jerk.
AI.conclusion: The new framework enhances internal simulator fidelity and overall control performance by incorporating physical structures and thermodynamic principles into the model.

title: Training-Free Occluded Text Rendering via Glyph Priors and Attention-Guided Semantic Blending
summary: We present a training-free framework for occluded text rendering with a pretrained FLUX.1-dev backbone. The task requires a model to render recognizable typography and place an occluding object over the intended text region. This setting remains difficult for existing text-to-image generators: the occluder often drifts away from the text, while the text may be distorted or appear to float on top of the occluding object. To address this problem, we propose a restarted dual-stream inference framework that decouples text-layout preservation from occluder insertion. A Base Stream provides a clean typographic reference and same-step key/value (K/V) features, while the Edit Stream is conditioned on the occlusion prompt. We further adopt the spectral glyph-prior idea from FreeText and adapt it to stabilize the target text structure during early-to-mid denoising. In the reasoning pass, our method localizes the target text, estimates a text-band region from token-conditioned attention and glyph support, and derives an anchor-aware hard fusion mask for the occluder. In the final edit pass, generation restarts from the same initial noise and applies hard mask-guided image-token K/V replacement at selected attention sites, preserving the Base layout outside the mask while injecting the occluder appearance from the Edit Stream inside the mask. Experiments on representative occluded text scenarios demonstrate substantially improved text readability and competitive occlusion alignment, yielding more stable object-on-text compositions without any model fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a training-free method for occluded text rendering using a pretrained model, enabling better stability in text readability and occlusion alignment without requiring model fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing text-to-image generators struggle with aligning occluders and preserving text integrity, leading to poor readability and misalignment.
AI.method: The proposed method utilizes a dual-stream framework separating text-layout preservation from occluder insertion, employing cleaned typographic references and a specific occlusion prompt.
AI.result: Experiments show improved readability and alignment in occluded text scenarios compared to existing methods, demonstrating stability in object-on-text compositions.
AI.conclusion: The framework achieves better occluded text rendering without the need for additional training, validating its efficacy in real-world applications.

title: How Loud Rumbles Hit Newsstands: A Data Analysis of Coverage and Spatial Bias in German News about Landslides Around the World
summary: Landslides often hit newsstands due to their destructive and potentially fatal effects. News are a valuable source of information for creating or enriching disaster databases and for expediting media-based studies of the dynamics of media attention. To accomplish that, news datasets must be filtered, geolocated and validated. This paper focuses on how landslides around the world are reported in German newspapers. We analyse almost 60k news articles about 5.5k news events in a 25-year period, compare it with external measures of countries' susceptibility to landslides and provide insights, e.g.~the overreporting of Southern and Western Europe, to foment further studies on inequalities in media attention to international disasters.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes how landslides are reported in German newspapers, examining 60,000 articles over 25 years to highlight disparities in media attention across regions.
AI.motivation: To enhance disaster databases and understand media dynamics related to landslide reporting, particularly in German newspapers.
AI.method: The study reviews nearly 60,000 news articles covering 5,500 landslide events over a 25-year timeframe, analyzing them in relation to countries' susceptibility to landslides.
AI.result: Findings reveal a tendency to overreport landslides in Southern and Western Europe, suggesting disparities in media attention.
AI.conclusion: The paper advocates for further research into media attention inequalities regarding international disasters.

title: Convex Dataset Valuation for Post-Training
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICML '26. 30 pages, 8 figures
summary: Improving LLM performance on downstream tasks sometimes requires leveraging auxiliary datasets during post-training. In practice, however, developers face constraints on compute, labeling, and licensing costs that preclude using all available data, necessitating principled dataset-level selection. These constraints are increasingly shaped by dataset marketplaces, where data acquisition is governed by budgets and negotiation. We study dataset valuation as a subset selection problem during LLM post-training. Our goal is to identify and weight auxiliary datasets so as to maximize target task performance given constrained budgets. We first show that commonly used gradient alignment scores provide a reasonable yet incomplete valuation signal, as they ignore redundancy among datasets. To address this, we propose a scalable convex dataset-level valuation method based on kernel mean matching (KMM) in gradient space, which jointly accounts for alignment with the target task and redundancy across auxiliary datasets. Through extensive experiments across diverse post-training settings and tasks, we show that our approach consistently outperforms existing valuation baselines, achieving stronger performance with low computational overhead. Our results position dataset valuation as a practical decision tool for post-training data selection in market-constrained large language model settings. The code is available at https://github.com/uiuctml/convex_data_valuation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method for selecting auxiliary datasets to improve LLM performance under budget constraints, addressing limitations of existing valuation methods.
AI.motivation: To enhance LLM performance while addressing constraints related to compute, labeling, and licensing costs in data selection.
AI.method: A scalable convex dataset-level valuation technique based on kernel mean matching (KMM) in gradient space, accounting for both task alignment and redundancy among datasets.
AI.result: The proposed method outperforms existing valuation baselines in various post-training scenarios with low computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: Dataset valuation is a practical tool for effective post-training data selection in market-constrained LLM contexts.

title: Observation-Aligned Mask Priors for Learning Physical Dynamics from Authentic Occlusions
summary: Learning physical dynamics directly from incomplete observations is challenging because authentic occlusions are structured, sample-dependent, and often missing not at random, whereas existing methods typically rely on heuristic masking rules or predefined mask distributions. We propose Observation-Aligned Mask Priors, a framework that learns the distribution of authentic observation masks and uses it to construct context-query partitions for training from incomplete data. Specifically, we pretrain a Bayesian Flow Network (BFN) on binary observation masks to capture real occlusion topologies, then guide BFN sampling with a globally normalized cross-entropy objective to generate sample-specific masks aligned with each sparse observation. The intersection between the guided mask and the observed mask defines the context, and the remaining observed entries become query targets for a diffusion-based reconstruction model. We show that this intersection-based partitioning gives every valid observed dimension a strictly positive probability of being queried, preventing zero-query dead zones and local generative collapse. Experiments on three real-world oceanographic datasets with authentic satellite occlusions, across resolutions up to 256$\times$256, show consistent improvements over strong diffusion baselines in MSE and PSNR. These results demonstrate that learning mask priors from authentic occlusions is an effective alternative to heuristic masking for learning from incomplete physical observations without access to fully observed fields.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Observation-Aligned Mask Priors, a framework that learns from incomplete observations by generating sample-specific masks to improve physical dynamics learning.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of learning physical dynamics from incomplete data due to structured and sample-dependent occlusions that traditional methods cannot effectively handle.
AI.method: Pretrain a Bayesian Flow Network on binary observation masks to model real occlusion topologies; use guided sampling with a cross-entropy objective to create aligned observation masks for reconstruction.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate significant improvements in MSE and PSNR on three oceanographic datasets with real satellite occlusions, showcasing the effectiveness of sample-specific mask generation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method offers an effective solution for learning from incomplete physical observations, outperforming existing heuristic masking techniques.

title: Isolating Nonlinear Independent Sources in fMRI with $β$-TCVAE Models
summary: Learning meaningful latent representations from nonlinear fMRI data remains a fundamental challenge in neuroimaging analysis. Traditional independent component analysis, widely used due to its ability to estimate interpretable functional brain networks, relies on a linear mixing assumption for latent sources, limiting its ability to capture the inherently nonlinear and complex organization of brain dynamics. More recently, deep representation learning methods have emerged as promising alternatives for modeling nonlinear latent structure. However, many of these approaches have been evaluated primarily on simulated datasets or natural image benchmarks, with comparatively limited validation on real-world neuroimaging data such as fMRI. In this work, we are motivated by the $β$-TCVAE (Total Correlation Variational Autoencoder), a refinement of the $β$-VAE framework for learning latent representations without introducing additional hyperparameters during training. We adapt and modify this model to fMRI data for nonlinear source disentanglement, aiming to separate mixed spatial and temporal brain signals into interpretable components. We show that the $β$-TCVAE framework can recover meaningful nonlinear spatial components with biological relevance, including well-established intrinsic connectivity networks such as the default mode network. Furthermore, we evaluate the learned representations using functional network connectivity, showing that the latent structure captures coherent and interpretable brain organization patterns. This study provides a pilot investigation that bridges nonlinear representation learning and fMRI analysis.

AI.tldr: This study explores the application of the β-TCVAE framework for extracting meaningful latent representations from nonlinear fMRI data, overcoming limitations of traditional methods.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the challenge of learning meaningful latent representations from nonlinear fMRI data, where traditional methods fall short due to linearity assumptions.
AI.method: The authors adapt the β-TCVAE (Total Correlation Variational Autoencoder) for fMRI data to achieve nonlinear source disentanglement and interpretable representations.
AI.result: The β-TCVAE framework successfully recovers biologically relevant nonlinear spatial components and demonstrates coherence in learned representations through functional network connectivity.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully bridges nonlinear representation learning and fMRI analysis, providing a foundation for future research in this area.

title: How Good LLMs Are at Answering Bangla Medical Visual Questions? Dataset and Benchmarking
comment: 14 pages, 7 figures, 5 tables, Proceedings of The Second AAAI Bridge Program on AI for Medicine and Healthcare, PMLR 317:1-14, 2026
summary: Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) and Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) have enabled general-purpose systems to demonstrate promising capabilities in complex reasoning tasks, including those in the medical domain. Medical Visual Question Answering (MedVQA) has particularly benefited from these developments. However, despite Bangla being one of the most widely spoken languages globally, there exists no established MedVQA benchmark for it. To address this gap, we introduce BanglaMedVQA, a dataset comprising clinically validated image-question-answer pairs, along with a comprehensive evaluation of current foundation models on this resource. Consistent with prior findings that report low performance of current models on English MedVQA benchmarks, our analysis reveals that Bangla performance is substantially lower, reflecting the challenges inherent to low-resource languages. Even top-performing models such as Gemini and GPT-4.1 mini fail to accurately answer specialized diagnostic questions, indicating severe limitations in fine-grained medical reasoning. Although certain open-source models, such as Gemma-3, occasionally outperform these models in general categories, they too struggle with clinically complex questions, underscoring the urgent need for top-notch evaluation method.

AI.tldr: BanglaMedVQA introduces a new dataset for Medical Visual Question Answering in Bangla, highlighting low performance of models on clinically relevant questions compared to English benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To create a benchmark for MedVQA in Bangla, addressing the lack of resources for this widely spoken language in medical reasoning tasks.
AI.method: The dataset consists of clinically validated image-question-answer pairs, which are evaluated against current foundation models.
AI.result: Current models exhibit poor performance on the BanglaMedVQA dataset, with severe limitations in addressing specialized diagnostic questions despite some models performing better in general categories.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight the need for improved evaluation methods and resources for low-resource languages in complex medical reasoning tasks.

title: ESI-Bench: Towards Embodied Spatial Intelligence that Closes the Perception-Action Loop
summary: Spatial intelligence unfolds through a perception-action loop: agents act to acquire observations, and reason about how observations vary as a function of action. Rather than passively processing what is seen, they actively uncover what is unseen - occluded structure, dynamics, containment, and functionality that cannot be resolved from passive sensing alone. We move beyond prior formulations of spatial intelligence that assume oracle observations by recasting the observer as an actor. We introduce ESI-BENCH, a comprehensive benchmark for embodied spatial intelligence spanning 10 task categories and 29 subcategories built on OmniGibson, grounded in Spelke's core knowledge systems. Agents must decide what abilities to deploy - perception, locomotion, and manipulation - and how to sequence them to actively accumulate task-relevant evidence. We conduct extensive experiments on state-of-the-art MLLMs and find that active exploration substantially outperforms passive counterparts, with agents spontaneously discovering emergent spatial strategies without explicit instructions, while random multi-view often adds noise rather than signal despite consuming far more images. Most failures stem not from weak perception but from action blindness: poor action choices lead to poor observations, which in turn drive cascading errors. While explicit 3D grounding stabilizes reasoning on depth-sensitive tasks, imperfect 3D representation proves more harmful than 2D baselines by distorting spatial relations. Human studies further reveal that unlike humans who seek falsifying viewpoints and revise beliefs under contradiction, models commit prematurely with high confidence regardless of evidence quality, exposing a metacognitive gap that neither better perception nor more embodied interaction alone can close.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ESI-BENCH, a benchmark for evaluating embodied spatial intelligence that emphasizes active exploration over passive observation, revealing emergent spatial strategies and addressing action blindness in performance.
AI.motivation: To enhance understanding of spatial intelligence by framing agents as active participants in uncovering unseen structures through perception and action.
AI.method: The authors developed ESI-BENCH, a benchmark involving 10 task categories and 29 subcategories, and conducted experiments on state-of-the-art multi-layered learning models (MLLMs) to evaluate performance in spatial tasks.
AI.result: Active exploration of spatial environments outperforms passive observation approaches; agents demonstrated emergent strategies but also faced failures due to poor action choices affecting observations.
AI.conclusion: A metacognitive gap exists between models and human strategies in spatial reasoning, indicating that simply improving perception and interaction is insufficient for addressing this gap.

title: EmoMind: Decoding Affective Captions from Human Brain fMRI
summary: Decoding visual experience from brain activity has advanced substantially, but cur- rent brain-to-text systems largely recover semantic content while discarding affect. Additionally, language models can generate emotional text when prompted with categorical labels, but such labels collapse rich inter-subject variability into coarse discrete bins. We present EmoMind, the first end-to-end pipeline for decoding affective captions directly from fMRI signals. EmoMind first retrieves a semanti- cally grounded neutral scene description from brain-decoded visual features, then rewrites it using a continuous 34-dimensional emotion vector decoded from the same fMRI recording. To control the balance between content preservation and affective expression, we train the rewriter with classifier-free guidance against an identity-preserving null branch, enabling smooth interpolation between semantic fidelity and affective expressivity. We evaluate affective caption generation with a three-axis validation framework spanning subject-specificity, structural geometry, and causal control. We further augment this framework with a synthetic-brain substitution test that probes robustness to the measurement apparatus, and we benchmark each axis against GPT-4 prompted with brain-decoded top-5 emotion labels as a strong discrete baseline. Across two independent emotion fMRI datasets, EmoMind significantly outperforms label-prompted GPT-4 on all three axes, with the largest gains on metrics that require person-specific affective structure rather than population-level emotion aggregation. These results establish continuous brain-decoded affect as a viable control signal for individualized affective cap- tion generation and open new directions for studying individual affective brain organisation.

AI.tldr: EmoMind is a new system that decodes affective captions from fMRI brain signals, improving upon existing models by integrating continuous emotional data.
AI.motivation: To enhance brain-to-text systems by incorporating affective content alongside semantic understanding.
AI.method: EmoMind retrieves neutral scene descriptions from brain-decoded visual features and rewrites them using a continuous 34-dimensional emotion vector, utilizing classifier-free guidance for balance.
AI.result: EmoMind significantly outperforms GPT-4 in generating affective captions, particularly in metrics reflecting individual emotional structure.
AI.conclusion: EmoMind proves the effectiveness of continuous brain-decoded emotions for personalized affective caption generation and suggests new avenues for exploring individual emotional brain organization.

title: Coarse Semantic Injection for LLM-Conditioned Structured Indoor Prediction
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have recently been used as structured decoders for indoor understanding from 3D point-token inputs. However, point cloud encoders often under-represent thin structural elements such as doors and windows after voxelization and sparse pooling, and may miss individual furniture instances in cluttered scenes. We propose an interface-preserving semantic augmentation for LLM-conditioned structured decoding. The key idea is to associate semantic evidence with the point-cloud representation, reduce it to a coarse four-group code (furniture, walls, openings, and others), and encode it as an RGBB point interface: red for furniture, green for walls, blue for openings, and black for others, where RGBB denotes four semantic color states represented in three RGB channels rather than an additional fourth channel. This semantic color code is appended to the original raw point attributes before tokenization, so geometry and semantics share the same sparse tokenization path while the downstream language model decoder and output serialization remain unchanged. We further introduce a lightweight routed semantic shift module, with an auxiliary head used only for training-time ratio/budget regularization and analysis, to strengthen semantic cues after sparse pooling. The overall pipeline can use RGB-derived semantic evidence. Under these controlled semantic-source settings, the reported metrics improve across Structured3D, the SpatialLM dataset, and ARKitScenes, especially for opening localization and per-instance furniture detection in cluttered scenes. Ablations clarify the roles of semantic source, color coding, token fusion, and shift injection, while also showing that color/entropy effects remain nontrivial.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an innovative approach to enhance indoor scene understanding using LLMs by addressing the limitations of point cloud encoders in representing thin structures and cluttered furniture.
AI.motivation: To improve the representation of thin structural elements and individual furniture instances in point clouds, which are often inadequately captured by traditional encoding methods.
AI.method: The authors introduce interface-preserving semantic augmentation that associates semantic evidence with a point-cloud representation, utilizing a coarse four-group code (furniture, walls, openings, others) encoded in an RGBB format for improved tokenization.
AI.result: The proposed method shows improved performance metrics across several datasets, particularly in opening localization and per-instance furniture detection, while integrating semantic color coding with sparse tokenization.
AI.conclusion: The results demonstrate that combining geometry with semantic evidence in this structured decoding approach leads to significant enhancements in cluttered scene analysis, supporting the effectiveness of the proposed semantic shift module.

title: Learning Relative Representations for Fine-Grained Multimodal Alignment with Limited Data
summary: Multimodal pre-training demonstrates strong generalization performance, but this paradigm is often impractical in domains where paired data are scarce. A promising alternative is post-hoc multimodal alignment, which aligns separately pre-trained unimodal encoders using a limited number of paired examples. However, existing methods focus primarily on aligning global representations, missing patch-token relations. This may hinder transfer to tasks that require fine-grained cross-modal matching beyond coarse sample-level semantics. To address this issue, we propose a post-hoc alignment method that learns token-level cross-modal structure using relative representations. Specifically, we represent images and texts through their token-level similarities to a set of learnable anchors in each modality space, which are trained to induce consistent cross-modal similarity patterns for matched pairs. Despite learning only the anchors without heavy projection layers, our approach consistently outperforms existing methods in zero-shot classification, cross-modal retrieval, and zero-shot segmentation by a substantial margin. This highlights the importance of modeling fine-grained cross-modal structure for effective post-hoc multimodal alignment with limited paired data.

AI.tldr: Proposes a post-hoc alignment method for multimodal learning that enhances fine-grained cross-modal matching using token-level similarities, outperforming existing approaches with less paired data.
AI.motivation: To improve multimodal alignment methods that struggle with fine-grained matching due to the focus on global representations and limited paired data.
AI.method: A post-hoc alignment technique that models token-level cross-modal structures using learnable anchors for capturing similarities between image and text tokens.
AI.result: Outperforming existing methods in zero-shot classification, cross-modal retrieval, and zero-shot segmentation, demonstrating enhanced performance despite limited training pairs.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained cross-modal structure modeling is crucial for effective post-hoc multimodal alignment when paired data is scarce.

title: iPOE: Interpretable Prompt Optimization via Explanations
summary: Prompt optimization has often been framed as a discrete search problem to find high-performing and robust instructions for an LLM. However, the search result might not make it transparent why and where specific prompt changes lead to performance gains. This is in contrast to how humans are instructed for annotation tasks. Here, researchers carefully design annotation guidelines, leading to enhanced annotation consistency. Our paper aims at joining these two approaches and introduces iPOE, a novel interpretable prompt optimization strategy via explanations. We guide the prompt optimization process by automatically created guidelines from explanations of annotation decisions (either automatically generated or from humans). This set of guidelines is furthermore optimized by as series of operations, including removing, adding, shuffling, and merging. The resulting prompt includes guidelines that instruct the annotation, making the decision process of the LLM and the optimization transparent. It therefore supports also laypeople in the area of prompt optimization, particularly in challenging domains requiring expertise. In our experiments on four datasets, we find that iPOE can improves over prompts without guidelines and with random selected guidelines by up to $31\%$ and $35\%$, respectively. Moreover, LLM explanations can replace human explanations in the proposed method.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces iPOE, a novel interpretable prompt optimization strategy that uses automated guidelines from explanation of annotation decisions, enhancing performance and transparency in prompt optimization for LLMs.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between discrete search methods for prompt optimization and human instruction practices that emphasize clarity and consistency in annotation tasks.
AI.method: iPOE utilizes automatically generated or human-derived explanations to create guidelines that optimize the prompt by applying operations like removing, adding, shuffling, and merging.
AI.result: iPOE shows improvements in prompt performance, achieving up to 31% better results over prompts without guidelines and 35% over those with randomly selected guidelines across four datasets.
AI.conclusion: The methodology provides transparency in the decision-making process of LLMs and demonstrates that LLM-generated explanations can effectively substitute human annotations in guiding prompt optimization.

title: Propagation of Chaos in Contextual Flow Maps
summary: We develop a quantitative statistical theory of transformers in the large-context regime by adopting the abstraction of contextual flow maps (CFMs): dynamical systems that evolve a distinguished token in the presence of a contextual measure across a stack of attention blocks. Within this framework, the finite-context model approximates an idealized infinite-context system in which the contextual measure is replaced by its underlying population, so that the context length $n$ becomes a statistical resource. Exploiting the McKean--Vlasov structure of the dynamics and the classical machinery of propagation of chaos, we establish a forward bound controlling the deviation between the finite- and infinite-context CFMs uniformly along depth, and a backward bound controlling the deviation between the corresponding training trajectories uniformly across iterations of online gradient descent. Both bounds achieve the optimal Wasserstein rate $n^{-1/d}$ for general CFMs and parametric rate $n^{-1/2}$ for a restricted class of CFMs that includes transformers as a special case. The analysis rests on a new Eulerian adjoint formulation of the loss gradient and stability estimates for the resulting forward--adjoint system, both of which may be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a quantitative theory for transformers using contextual flow maps, demonstrating bounds on deviation between finite and infinite-context systems and establishing optimal rates using statistical methods.
AI.motivation: To understand the dynamics of transformers in large-context scenarios and improve the effectiveness of training procedures by modeling context as a statistical resource.
AI.method: Developed a framework using contextual flow maps, applying the McKean--Vlasov theory and propagation of chaos to derive bounds on deviations in transformer dynamics and training.
AI.result: Established forward and backward bounds for CFMs, achieving optimal Wasserstein rates and parametric rates for transformers, utilizing a new adjoint formulation of loss gradients.
AI.conclusion: The findings enhance the theoretical understanding of transformers' context handling, suggesting avenues for future research and applications in machine learning.

title: FOL2NS: Generating Natural Sentences from First-Order Logic
summary: Translating formal language into natural language is a foundational challenge in NLP, driving various downstream applications in semantic parsing, theorem validation, and question answering. In this study, we introduce First-Order Logic to Natural Sentence (FOL2NS), a neurosymbolic framework designed to generate synthetic FOL formulas and convert them into natural human expressions. It handles deeply nested structures with varying quantifier depths (QD), which are rarely captured by existing corpora. By combining rule-driven modules with fine-tuned language models, FOL2NS enhances the diversity and coverage of the generated samples. In our experiments, we systematically evaluate the framework's capabilities through both character-level analysis and overall performance metrics. Experimental results show that FOL2NS can reliably produce well-formed templates and fluent statements, but it faces challenges in achieving precise semantic representations and natural generation as structural complexity increases.

AI.tldr: FOL2NS is a neurosymbolic framework that translates First-Order Logic into natural language, improving diversity in generated outputs but struggling with complex structures.
AI.motivation: The study targets the challenge of converting formal language into natural language, crucial for various NLP applications.
AI.method: FOL2NS generates synthetic FOL formulas and translates them into natural language using a combination of rule-driven modules and fine-tuned language models.
AI.result: The framework produces well-formed templates and fluent natural language statements, though it encounters difficulties with complex semantic representations.
AI.conclusion: While FOL2NS shows promise in generating diverse natural language outputs, it needs improvements in semantic accuracy and handling structural complexity.

title: Thinking with Patterns: Breaking the Perceptual Bottleneck in Visual Planning via Pattern Induction
summary: Planning from raw visual input remains a significant challenge for current Vision-Language Models (VLMs), when the complexity of input is beyond their one-step perception capability. Motivated by recent advances in Thinking with Images (TWI), a reasonable solution is to decompose the perception process into simpler steps by iteratively acquiring and incorporating local visual evidence. However, even though current VLMs are well-trained in general TWI ability, their perceptual bottleneck in the planning domain remains. To tackle this challenge, we formulate TWI as a tool to gradually build and reflect an accurate internal world model. We find that the resulting training-free planning strategy enables VLMs to solve tasks that are far beyond their initial capabilities, at the cost that too many TWI operations would significantly increase the computational overhead. To further improve efficiency, we propose Pattern Inference, a novel TWI strategy enabling VLMs to actively recognize known visual patterns in the new tasks and directly infer local world model structures. To obtain these patterns, we propose Pattern Induction, an online inductive learning strategy treating visual patterns as composite and reusable experts, which are autonomously discovered and optimized from experience. Experimental evaluations in FrozenLake, Crafter and CubeBench domains show that our approaches achieve a desirable balance between accuracy and efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge of planning from complex visual inputs in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) by proposing an iterative perception process and a new strategy called Pattern Inference to enhance efficiency and accuracy in task-solving.
AI.motivation: To tackle the perceptual bottleneck in planning for VLMs when faced with complex visual inputs that exceed their one-step perception capabilities.
AI.method: The authors decompose the perception process into simpler steps using Thinking with Images, introducing Pattern Inference for recognizing visual patterns, and Pattern Induction for inductive learning of these patterns.
AI.result: The training-free planning strategy allowed VLMs to solve tasks beyond their initial capabilities while maintaining a desirable balance between accuracy and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approaches demonstrate significant improvements in task performance across various domains, suggesting the effectiveness of iterative visual evidence acquisition and pattern recognition strategies.

title: Leveraging Graph Structure in Seq2Seq Models for Knowledge Graph Link Prediction
comment: 9 pages, 1 figure, 2 tables. Preprint of a paper accepted at the 5th Workshop on LLM-Integrated Knowledge Graph Generation from Text (TEXT2KG), co-located with ESWC 2026, May 10--14, 2026, Dubrovnik, Croatia
summary: We introduce Graph-Augmented Sequence-to-Sequence (GA-S2S), a novel framework that integrates a T5-small encoder-decoder with a Relational Graph Attention Network (RGAT) to improve link prediction in knowledge graphs. While existing Seq2Seq models rely solely on surface-level textual descriptions of entities and relations and at best, flatten the neighborhoods of a query entity into a single linear sequence, thereby discarding the inherent graph structure, GA-S2S jointly encodes both textual features and the full $k$-hop subgraph topology surrounding the query entity. By integrating raw encoder outputs with RGAT's relation-aware embeddings, our model captures and leverages richer multi-hop relational patterns and textual information. Our preliminary experiments on the CoDEx dataset demonstrate that GA-S2S outperforms competitive Seq2Seq-based baseline models, achieving up to a 19\% relative gain in link prediction accuracy.

AI.tldr: GA-S2S is a novel framework that enhances link prediction in knowledge graphs by combining a T5-small encoder-decoder with a Relational Graph Attention Network for richer context encoding.
AI.motivation: To improve link prediction accuracy by overcoming limitations of existing Seq2Seq models that neglect graph structure.
AI.method: Integrates a T5-small encoder-decoder with a Relational Graph Attention Network, capturing both textual and k-hop subgraph features.
AI.result: GA-S2S outperforms baseline models on the CoDEx dataset, achieving up to a 19% improvement in link prediction accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively captures multi-hop relational patterns, enhancing link predictions by integrating graph topology with textual information.

title: Learning Unbiased Permutations via Flow Matching
summary: Learning permutations is fundamental to sorting, ranking, and matching, but existing differentiable methods based on entropy-regularized Sinkhorn produce a single softened solution and collapse under ambiguity. We present PermFlow, a conditional flow matching framework that operates directly on the affine subspace of matrices with unit row and column sums. A closed-form tangent-space projector preserves these constraints exactly along every trajectory, by construction rather than through iterative correction, and a nearest-target coupling routes distinct noisy initializations toward distinct valid permutations. The result is a model that captures multimodal permutation distributions rather than collapsing them to a single mode. On a visual sorting task with blended-digit ambiguity and a symmetric linear assignment problem, PermFlow achieves high accuracy on unambiguous inputs and recovers both valid permutations under ambiguity, where Sinkhorn-based baselines structurally fail.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PermFlow, a new framework for learning permutations that overcomes the limitations of previous methods by accurately handling multimodal distributions and maintaining constraint adherence.
AI.motivation: Existing differentiable methods for learning permutations struggle with ambiguity, often collapsing to a single solution, which limits their effectiveness in sorting and ranking tasks.
AI.method: PermFlow utilizes a conditional flow matching framework that operates on matrix affine subspaces with fixed row and column sums, employing a closed-form tangent-space projector to maintain constraints throughout the learning process.
AI.result: PermFlow outperforms Sinkhorn-based methods by effectively capturing multimodal permutation distributions, achieving high accuracy on visual sorting tasks and correctly identifying valid permutations in ambiguous situations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method successfully addresses the shortcomings of previous approaches, demonstrating significant improvements in scenarios involving ambiguous inputs.

title: VGGT-CD: Training-Free Robust Registration for 3D Change Detection
comment: 13 pages, 5 figures. Code is available at: https://github.com/WZ-CS/VGGT-CD
summary: 3D change detection from multi-view images is essential for urban monitoring, disaster assessment, and autonomous driving. However, existing methods predominantly operate in the 2D domain, where viewpoint variations are mistaken for physical changes and depth is unavailable. While visual geometry foundation models like VGGT rapidly produce dense point clouds from unposed images, independent per-epoch reconstruction encounters fundamental obstacles: unpredictable inter-epoch scale ambiguity, registration-change paradox where scene changes corrupt alignment, and pervasive edge-flying noise. To address these challenges, we present VGGT-CD, a training-free pipeline decoupling cross-temporal registration from dynamic-change interference. In the Coarse Stage, sparse keyframe joint inference establishes a unified metric space and yields an initial Sim(3) prior. In the Fine Stage, dense reconstructions are purified by isolating static-background correspondences. A closed-form centroid alignment refines the translation while locking scale and rotation, using a residual self-check to mathematically guarantee non-degradation. Evaluated on an 11-scene benchmark from the World Across Time dataset, VGGT-CD reduces Absolute Trajectory Error by 44% outdoors and 59% indoors. It completes registration over 6 times faster, producing high-purity 3D change maps without task-specific training.

AI.tldr: A new method, VGGT-CD, improves 3D change detection from multi-view images by addressing challenges in cross-temporal registration and dynamic changes without the need for training.
AI.motivation: To tackle the limitations of existing 2D methods in 3D change detection, particularly issues related to scale ambiguity and alignment corruption due to scene changes.
AI.method: VGGT-CD uses a two-stage approach: Coarse Stage for joint inference to establish a metric space and Fine Stage for purifying dense reconstructions while refining translation and locking scale and rotation.
AI.result: VGGT-CD achieves a 44% reduction in Absolute Trajectory Error outdoors and 59% indoors, while completing registration over 6 times faster than previous methods.
AI.conclusion: This method produces high-quality 3D change maps efficiently without requiring task-specific training, marking a significant advancement in the field.

title: Distinguishable Deletion: Unifying Knowledge Erasure and Refusal for Large Language Model Unlearning
summary: Mitigating sensitive and harmful outputs is fundamental to ensuring safe deployment of LLMs. Existing approaches typically follow two paradigms: Knowledge Deletion (KD), which erases undesirable information during training, and Distinguishable Refusal (DR), which steers models away from using sensitive knowledge during inference. Despite rapid progress, KD-based unlearning struggles with biased deletion due to suppressing specific token sequences as a substitute for complete knowledge removal, whereas DR-based unlearning risks the re-emergence of harmful knowledge because the underlying knowledge remains intact. To address these issues, we propose Distinguishable Deletion ($\mathrm{D^2}$), a paradigm that restricts the response distribution in the latent representation rather than specific tokens to erase undesirable knowledge, while distinguishing it from retained knowledge, enabling a refusal mechanism to handle unlearned inputs safely and coherently. To implement $\mathrm{D^2}$, we introduce an energy index that quantifies the presence of knowledge and the separation between unlearned and retained content. Mathematical and empirical analyses show that energy is both accurate and efficient, enabling Energy-based Unlearning Alignment (EUA) to enforce energy-boundary unlearning during training and apply an energy-based refusal mechanism at inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EUA significantly outperforms previous methods, indicating the superiority of $\mathrm{D^2}$. Our code is available at https://github.com/Puning97/EUA-for-LLM-Unlearning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new approach, Distinguishable Deletion ($\mathrm{D^2}$), to mitigate harmful outputs in language models by modifying latent response distributions instead of erasing specific tokens.
AI.motivation: Ensuring safe deployment of large language models (LLMs) necessitates the mitigation of sensitive outputs, an area with limitations in current methods of Knowledge Deletion and Distinguishable Refusal.
AI.method: The proposed $	ext{D}^2$ method modifies the distribution in latent representations to effectively delete undesired knowledge while distinguishing retained knowledge, employing an energy index for quantification.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that Energy-based Unlearning Alignment (EUA) using $	ext{D}^2$ significantly outperforms traditional methods in unlearning harmful information.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the effectiveness of $	ext{D}^2}$ in addressing limitations of existing unlearning techniques, promising improvements in LLM safety.

title: Context Memorization for Efficient Long Context Generation
summary: Modern large language model (LLM) applications increasingly rely on long conditioning prefixes to control model behavior at inference time. While prefix-augmented inference is effective, it incurs two structural limitations: i) the prefix's influence fades as generation proceeds, and ii) attention computation over the prefix scales linearly with its length. Existing approaches either keep the prefix in attention while compressing it, or internalize it into model parameters through gradient-based training. The former still attends to the prefix at inference, while the latter is training-intensive and ill-suited to prefix updates. To address these issues, we propose attention-state memory, a training-free approach that externalizes the prefix into a lightweight, lookup-based memory of precomputed attention states between prefix and query tokens. On ManyICLBench with LLaMA-3.1-8B, our method improves accuracy over in-context learning at 1K-8K memory budgets while reducing attention latency by 1.36x at 8K, and surpasses full-attention RAG performance on NBA benchmark using only 20% of its memory footprint.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces attention-state memory to enhance long prefix conditioning in LLMs, addressing limitations in current methods by externalizing prefix influence for improved accuracy and reduced latency.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to overcome structural limitations of existing LLM methods that rely on long conditioning prefixes, specifically addressing the fading influence of prefixes and inefficient attention computations as prefix length increases.
AI.method: The proposed approach, attention-state memory, externalizes the prefix into a lightweight, lookup-based memory that holds precomputed attention states between prefix and query tokens, thus eliminating the need for continuous prefix attention during inference.
AI.result: On ManyICLBench with LLaMA-3.1-8B, the method improves accuracy for in-context learning with memory budgets of 1K-8K and reduces attention latency by 1.36x at 8K, while outperforming full-attention RAG on the NBA benchmark using only 20% of its memory footprint.
AI.conclusion: Attention-state memory provides a training-free solution that effectively utilizes prefixes in LLMs, offering significant improvements in both performance and efficiency.

title: Prefix-Adaptive Block Diffusion for Efficient Document Recognition
summary: Block Diffusion Models (BDMs) support parallel generation, flexible-length output, and KV caching, making them promising for efficient document parsing. However, existing BDMs bind denoising and cache commitment to fixed block boundaries: parallelism shrinks during intra-block denoising, while generated tokens cannot be cached until the whole block is completed. Moreover, intra-block bidirectional denoising conflicts with inter-block autoregression, creating inconsistent information flow that can challenge structure-sensitive recognition. We propose the Prefix-Adaptive Block Diffusion Model (PA-BDM), which replaces intra-block bidirectional denoising with causal denoising from prefix to suffix and treats the block size as a maximum candidate range rather than a fixed commitment unit. PA-BDM uses Confidence-gated Structural Loss (CSL) to build low-entropy prefixes before extending training to longer continuations. During inference, Progressive Prefix Commitment (PPC) then dynamically commits the longest reliable prefix into the KV cache and resets the next candidate range from the updated prefix, restoring a large parallel decoding space at each step. Experiments show that the 3B PA-BDM achieves higher recognition scores on several benchmarks and improves inference throughput by 71.6\% over the 2.5B MinerU-Diffusion.

AI.tldr: The Prefix-Adaptive Block Diffusion Model (PA-BDM) enhances Block Diffusion Models by enabling adaptive denoising and dynamic cache commitments for improved document parsing efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address limitations in existing Block Diffusion Models regarding fixed block boundaries that hinder parallelism and lead to information flow inconsistencies during document parsing.
AI.method: PA-BDM implements causal denoising instead of bidirectional denoising within blocks and employs Confidence-gated Structural Loss (CSL) to create low-entropy prefixes, along with Progressive Prefix Commitment (PPC) for dynamic cache updates.
AI.result: PA-BDM significantly outperforms existing models, achieving higher recognition scores and a 71.6% increase in inference throughput compared to the 2.5B MinerU-Diffusion model.
AI.conclusion: The proposed model effectively restores large parallel decoding spaces while ensuring better structural recognition, thereby advancing the capabilities of document parsing models.

title: Lever: Speculative LLM Inference on Smartphones
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly needed for interactive mobile applications, but high-quality models exceed the limited DRAM available on smartphones. Flash storage can hold larger models, yet flash-backed inference is slow because autoregressive decoding repeatedly invokes the target model and incurs costly I/O. We observe that speculative decoding is a natural fit for this setting: a small draft model can remain in DRAM, while a larger flash-resident target model verifies multiple candidate tokens per invocation. However, existing methods assume server-class accelerators and fail to account for prolonged I/O latency, limited computation parallelism, and irregular speculation execution.
  We present Lever, an end-to-end system for efficient flash-backed LLM inference on smartphones. Lever jointly optimizes the three stages of speculative decoding under mobile constraints. For drafting, it builds token trees using an I/O- and compute-aware gain-cost objective. For verification, it prunes low-value branches through early-exit prediction to reduce target-model computation. For execution, it maps speculation efficiently across mobile CPU-NPU hardware to improve utilization. Comprehensive evaluations show that Lever reduces inference latency by an average of 2.93x over baseline flash-offloaded inference and 1.50x over conventional speculative decoding, narrowing the latency gap between flash-backed and memory-resident LLM inference.

AI.tldr: Lever is a system designed for efficient inference of large language models (LLMs) on smartphones, utilizing speculative decoding to optimize performance under mobile constraints.
AI.motivation: The need for large language models in mobile applications is hindered by DRAM limitations on smartphones, necessitating the use of larger flash-resident models while addressing I/O latency and computation efficiency.
AI.method: Lever optimizes three stages of speculative decoding: drafting token trees with a gain-cost objective, pruning branches via early-exit prediction during verification, and efficiently mapping speculation across mobile hardware during execution.
AI.result: Lever achieves a 2.93x reduction in inference latency compared to baseline flash-offloaded inference and a 1.50x improvement over traditional speculative decoding methods.
AI.conclusion: Lever effectively narrows the latency gap between flash-backed and memory-resident LLM inference, making high-quality LLMs feasible for interactive smartphone applications.

title: The Unlearnability Phenomenon in RLVR for Language Models
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reward (RLVR) has proven effective in improving Large Language Model's (LLM) reasoning ability. However, the learning dynamics of RLVR remain underexplored. In this paper, we reveal a counterintuitive phenomenon: among hard examples that the model initially struggles with, a substantial subset remains unlearnable even when correct rollouts are present. To understand the phenomenon, we first demonstrate that existing optimization and sampling techniques fail to resolve unlearnability. With cross-example gradient analysis, we show that unlearnable examples have fundamental representation issue, characterized by low gradient similarity with the rest of the examples and ungeneralizable reasoning patterns. We further show that representation flaws are difficult to mitigate in RL, as data augmentation does not improve gradient similarity. Our study provides the first systematic characterization of unlearnable data in RLVR training and reveals fundamental limitations in current RL approaches for reasoning tasks. Code and data are available at \url{https://github.com/yulinchen99/unlearnability-rlvr}.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the phenomenon of unlearnable examples in RLVR training for LLMs, revealing that existing methods fail to address this issue due to fundamental representation deficiencies.
AI.motivation: To explore the unlearning dynamics in RLVR and identify the nature of unlearnable data in reasoning tasks.
AI.method: The authors analyze unlearnable examples through cross-example gradient analysis, comparing gradient similarity among examples and evaluating the effectiveness of sampling techniques.
AI.result: A significant number of hard examples remain unlearnable despite correct rollouts; issues stem from low gradient similarity and ungeneralizable reasoning patterns.
AI.conclusion: The paper highlights critical limitations in current RL methods for reasoning tasks, suggesting that representation issues are hard to overcome with typical data augmentation approaches.

title: Metric-Guided Feature Fusion of Visual Foundation Models for Segmentation Tasks
comment: Accepted to the CVPR 2026 Findings Track
summary: Although large-scale visual foundation models (VFMs) achieve remarkable performance in semantic understanding, they still underperform in instance-aware dense prediction tasks. They exhibit different biases in representation: for instance, promptable segmentation models (e.g., SAM2) focus on fine-grained region boundaries, while self-supervised models (e.g., DINOv3) emphasize object-level structure. This observation highlights the potential of combining complementary features from different VFMs to enhance downstream dense prediction tasks. However, naive multi-VFM fusion seldom leads to reliable gains, and interpretable principles for leveraging their complementary features are still underexplored. In this work, we propose a metric-guided approach that effectively selects and aggregates complementary features from different VFMs based on explicit assessment scores. Specifically, we design a suite of label-free metrics in feature space across two aspects, Structural Coherence and Edge Fidelity, to assess features of VFM encoders. Guided by these scores, we identify complementary edge-strong and structure-strong encoder pairs, and integrate them via a master-auxiliary fusion scheme. This feature fusion requires no complex architectural changes and is trained only in a single stage. Our model shows consistent performance gains across multiple dense prediction tasks compared with the baselines, with better object-level semantics and more accurately localized boundaries. The code is available at {https://github.com/gyc-code/metric-guided-fusion}.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a metric-guided approach to combine complementary features from different visual foundation models (VFMs) to improve performance in dense prediction tasks, addressing the limitations of naive multi-VFM fusion.
AI.motivation: To enhance the performance of instance-aware dense prediction tasks by combining the strengths of various VFMs, which exhibit different biases in representation.
AI.method: A metric-guided approach that uses label-free metrics assessing Structural Coherence and Edge Fidelity to select and aggregate features from various VFMs, integrated using a master-auxiliary fusion scheme.
AI.result: The proposed method demonstrates consistent performance improvements in dense prediction tasks, yielding better object-level semantics and more accurately localized boundaries compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The metric-guided feature fusion technique enhances VFM performance without requiring complex architectural changes, proving effective in real-world applications.

title: Multilingual jailbreaking of LLMs using low-resource languages
